intro_text:
Nazaré is a fishing village on the Portuguese Atlantic coast where the seabed drops five thousand meters straight down less than a kilometer from shore, and every winter the world's tallest rideable waves break directly in front of a 16th-century fortress on a cliff.

The beach that made Nazaré globally famous is **Praia do Norte** — the north beach — a 1.5 km stretch of sand directly below the **Forte de São Miguel Arcanjo**, the white lighthouse at the tip of the Sítio headland. Until 2010, nobody outside Portugal had heard of it. In November 2011, Hawaiian surfer Garrett McNamara rode a wave here measured at 78 feet (23.77 m). The photograph — McNamara, a speck at the base of a wall of water with the lighthouse visible to one side — is now one of the most-reproduced images in surfing history, and Nazaré is now the site of every current big-wave world record.

The physics are specific and unusual. The **Nazaré Canyon** — Canhão da Nazaré — is a 230-kilometer-long submarine trench that begins 500 m off this stretch of beach and runs west to the abyssal plain 5,000 meters below. When Atlantic winter swells sweep east toward Iberia, they **split** as they cross the canyon's edge — one part runs along the shallow bank north of the canyon, the other runs down the deep canyon trough. The two halves re-converge just offshore from Praia do Norte and arrive in phase, **focused**, amplified by refraction, and reshaped by the abrupt 95-meter-to-zero bathymetry at the shoreline. What lands on the beach is a wave that shouldn't exist anywhere else on the Atlantic coast. Physicists at the University of Lisbon have published on the mechanism. Surfers have simply learned to read the swell window.

But Nazaré is not a surf town that happens to have a village. **It is a village that, reluctantly at first and then deliberately, made itself a surf town.** The settlement at the base of the cliff — Praia, the lower town — has been a working fishing port since at least the 12th century. The upper town on the clifftop — **Sítio** — was founded around a chapel built in 1182 to house a Marian statue that, according to the Lenda da Nazaré, stopped the local nobleman Dom Fuas Roupinho's horse from plunging off the cliff during a foggy hunt. The church is still there. The pilgrimage every September 8 is still enormous. The fishermen still sail; the fishermen's wives still dry fish on wooden racks on the village beach; some of the older women still wear the traditional seven skirts (**sete saias**) that were the everyday dress of Nazarené women through the 1960s. All of this coexists with the surfers, the camera drones, the Canyon Run water-taxis, and the tourist buses.

**You can stand at the lighthouse, December through March, and watch it.** The Forte de São Miguel Arcanjo — built 1577, turned into a lighthouse in the 19th century, converted into a surf museum in 2017 — sits directly above where the biggest sets break. Admission is €2. From the ramparts you can see the entire Praia do Norte, the swell lines coming in from the west, the jet-ski drivers towing surfers into waves that are, often, taller than the fortress you are standing on. In a single afternoon in a good winter week, you can see the world's best big-wave surfers in the water, the Our Lady of Nazaré in her 12th-century chapel, and the fishermen's widows selling sun-dried sardinha a few hundred meters down the cliff. Almost nowhere else on Earth do three such distinct centuries inhabit the same kilometer.

Nazaré is, at this moment, one of the most written-about small-town stories on Earth — the HBO docuseries *100 Foot Wave* has run for three seasons; the WSL (World Surf League) runs its Big Wave season here; Portugal's national tourism agency has built the entire country's international marketing around the image of the wave against the lighthouse. The village has accepted this, rearranged itself for it, and is still here the other nine months of the year. **What you get, if you come, depends entirely on when.**
favela_note:
The tension at Nazaré is what happens to a 900-year-old Portuguese fishing village when the world decides it is suddenly the most interesting beach on Earth.

The numbers are specific: annual visitor count at Nazaré rose from **roughly 600,000 in 2010** (per the Portuguese Tourism Board's Leiria statistics) to over **2 million in 2023**. The fishing-fleet registered in Nazaré's port, by contrast, has continued its centuries-long contraction — fewer than 80 active vessels in 2023, down from over 400 in the 1960s. The Atlantic fishery has been in decline throughout the EU; Nazaré's decline is structural and older than the surf boom. But the **surf economy has arrived into a village that was already losing one of its two economic anchors**, and the result is specific: short-term-let apartments where fishermen's families used to live, seven-euro bicas at seafront cafés where the coffee is priced for Brazilian filmmakers, and a traffic pattern on Avenida Marginal that the village was never designed for.

The local government — **Câmara Municipal de Nazaré** — has done more than most small municipalities to protect the village's texture. The **Sítio** clifftop has been designated a heritage zone; new construction requires approval; the **Museu do Surf de Nazaré** (inside the Forte) was deliberately built to centralize the surf-tourism revenue into municipal rather than private hands. The **fisherwomen** — the women in the seven-skirt dress who sell fish on the Praia da Nazaré — are a protected cultural practice. The traditional sun-drying of sardines (*estendal*) is now a registered intangible-heritage practice of the region.

None of this undoes the change. A young person who grew up in Nazaré today will face a housing market priced for global tourists, a job market that increasingly requires English, and a cultural environment where their grandmother's dress and their uncle's fishing expertise are both simultaneously museums and present-tense practices. The surfers are not leaving; the swells are, if anything, **getting bigger** with the climate-shifted Atlantic.

The honest version of what to do as a visitor is simple: **stay overnight, spend money in places that employ locals rather than absentee owners, buy dried fish at the estendal if you eat fish, tip generously at the cafés, learn five Portuguese words**, and know that you are a guest in a place that has been working for 900 years and could, in one more generation, stop working. Nazaré is spectacular. It is also precarious. Those are the same fact.

day_in_time.dawn: The fishermen at the Praia da Nazaré port — the village's harbour, on the south side of the headland — are out before dawn if the weather allows. The men who surf Praia do Norte are parking their jet-skis at the Forte launch at first light. The Atlantic at Nazaré in December, 8 a.m., is a specific shade of grey that is almost blue and almost not. Surfers in 5mm wetsuits do gear checks from the cliff-top parking. The lighthouse is still lit. Coffee at **O Pirata** on Avenida da República opens at 6 a.m. for the fishing-fleet crews.
day_in_time.midday: From 11 a.m. — if it's a swell day — the cliff top at the Forte fills with spectators. Drones over the water. Camera setups the size of small cars. In-the-know locals bring lawn chairs and thermoses. The surfers are maybe two hundred meters offshore, looking from here the size of pin-heads against waves that make the lighthouse look small. The shouts from the crowd when someone rides a set wave are audible from the Praia do Norte parking lot. Down in the village, Rua das Flores has the lunch crowd. The fisherwomen are setting out the afternoon's drying racks of sardines.
day_in_time.golden: Late afternoon in winter, the sun is already low, and the light on the wave faces turns copper and then orange. The last session of the day is the money session — the famous photographs are mostly from the 4 p.m. to 5 p.m. window in December and January. The restaurants in Praia (lower town) begin to fill. **Taberna d'Adélia** has the first arrivals. The fisherwomen are packing up; the dried fish goes into storage. The funicular up to Sítio runs every ten minutes.
day_in_time.night: The Praia da Nazaré waterfront is lit. The old lighthouse is visible from the village. **Kiosks on Avenida Marginal** stay open for tourists; locals eat dinner at 8 p.m. sharp. The Sítio church courtyard is quiet — occasionally a small group returning from the 7 p.m. mass at the **Santuário de Nossa Senhora da Nazaré**. If there's a big-wave forecast the next day, the surfers are in bed by 10 p.m.; jet-ski training requires first light. Offshore the wind shifts, the canyon begins to do what the canyon does, and the swell that arrives at Praia do Norte at 6 a.m. tomorrow is already rolling past the Azores right now.

food_drink.name: Bacalhau à Brás / Bacalhau à Nazaré
food_drink.description: Nazaré's version of Portugal's national dish — salt cod flaked with shredded potato, eggs, olives, and parsley. Portugal has approximately 365 bacalhau recipes (one per day, the saying goes); the Nazaré variant leans drier and saltier, matching the village's 500-year relationship with dried cod from Newfoundland and the North Atlantic. €12–18.
food_drink.where: Taberna d'Adélia, Rosa dos Ventos, Casa Marques

food_drink.name: Sardinha seca (sun-dried sardines)
food_drink.description: The iconic Nazaré food. Sardines split, salted, and dried on wooden racks (*estendal*) directly on the Praia da Nazaré — the drying rack IS the village's most-photographed feature. Traditionally grilled whole over charcoal and eaten with broa (corn bread). **This is an intangible-heritage practice of the region.** €6–10 for a plate of three.
food_drink.where: Any fisherwoman on the estendal (Praia da Nazaré); most traditional restaurants

food_drink.name: Caldeirada de peixe
food_drink.description: Portuguese fish stew — often 4 or 5 species in a single pot: scorpion fish, hake, monkfish, squid, sometimes conger eel, with tomato, onion, white wine, potato, and saffron. €15–25. Best at family-run restaurants where the pot has been going for 40 years.
food_drink.where: A Casa Velha, Tasca do Celso (Praia)

food_drink.name: Vinho Verde & Ginjinha
food_drink.description: Vinho Verde is the young, slightly-fizzy white wine of northern Portugal — perfect for warm-weather lunches on the beach (€3–5 glass). Ginjinha (ginja) is sour cherry liqueur, Portugal's afternoon drink, often served in a tiny chocolate cup you eat after.
food_drink.where: Kiosks on Avenida Marginal; the Ginjinha Sem Rival is a standard

timeline.title: Lenda da Nazaré
timeline.description: According to local tradition, Dom Fuas Roupinho — the Portuguese nobleman in charge of the Knights Templar at nearby Porto de Mós — is hunting a deer through heavy fog when his horse stops dead at the edge of the Sítio cliff. The legend says the Virgin Mary, housed in a small chapel at the site, saved him from plunging into the Atlantic. He dismounts, gives thanks, and orders the founding of a larger sanctuary on the spot. This is the founding story of Sítio and the origin of the Nossa Senhora da Nazaré cult.

timeline.title: Santuário de Nossa Senhora da Nazaré consecrated
timeline.description: Construction of the current Marian sanctuary at the Sítio clifftop begins under King Ferdinand I. The church houses the Nossa Senhora da Nazaré statue, traditionally identified as a 4th-century Byzantine carving said to have been carried to Iberia from the Holy Land during the 8th-century Islamic conquests. Pilgrimages begin almost immediately.

timeline.title: Forte de São Miguel Arcanjo built
timeline.description: A small fortress built at the tip of the Sítio headland by Portuguese king Sebastião to defend the anchorage against Barbary and English corsairs. Named for the Archangel Michael. Will be modified through the 17th–19th centuries; converted into a lighthouse in 1903.

timeline.title: Rafael Bordalo Pinheiro paints the fisherwomen
timeline.description: Portuguese caricaturist and ceramicist Bordalo Pinheiro's paintings of Nazarené fisherwomen in their traditional seven-skirt dress become the iconic early-20th-century image of Portuguese coastal culture. His ceramic figures of these women are still made at the Fábrica de Faianças das Caldas da Rainha.

timeline.title: Funicular da Nazaré opens
timeline.description: A 318-meter inclined funicular links the lower village (Praia) to the upper village (Sítio) — a 120-meter vertical rise in 2 minutes. Replaces the old 175-step Capuchinhos stairway as the primary connection between the two parts of town. Still running; €2.20 round trip.

timeline.title: Dino Casimiro emails Garrett McNamara
timeline.description: Dino Casimiro — a local Nazarené bodyboarder — sends a series of emails to Hawaiian big-wave surfer Garrett McNamara with photos of the Praia do Norte waves, suggesting the place might be worth looking at. McNamara ignores the first several. Eventually responds. Travels to Nazaré in 2010 to see for himself. The small-town email correspondence is the origin of the modern big-wave era at Nazaré.

timeline.title: Garrett McNamara rides a 78-foot wave
timeline.description: On 1 November 2011, Hawaiian surfer Garrett McNamara is towed by jet-ski into a wave at Praia do Norte that is later measured at 23.77 m (78 ft). The Guinness Book of World Records certifies it as the largest wave ever surfed. The photograph of McNamara against the wave, with the Forte de São Miguel visible, goes globally viral. Nazaré becomes a big-wave destination within six months.

timeline.title: McNamara's second ride — possibly 100 feet
timeline.description: McNamara surfs another wave at Nazaré estimated visually at close to 100 feet, though the precise measurement is disputed. This wave is on the cover of Time, Newsweek, and Surfer Magazine. Nazaré's global brand as a big-wave destination is now secure.

timeline.title: Maya Gabeira's comeback ride
timeline.description: Brazilian big-wave surfer Maya Gabeira — nearly killed at Nazaré in 2013, in a wipeout that broke her fibula and required CPR from Carlos Burle on the beach — returns. Her 2018 wave at Nazaré is measured at 20.7m, the largest wave ever ridden by a woman. She extends her own record to 22.4m in 2020.

timeline.title: Museu do Surf de Nazaré opens at the Forte
timeline.description: The Forte de São Miguel Arcanjo is converted by the Câmara Municipal de Nazaré into a surf museum. Exhibits include surf equipment from the McNamara era, photographs of the biggest rides, and the bathymetric science of the canyon. Admission €2. Open year-round.

timeline.title: Rodrigo Koxa breaks the world record
timeline.description: Brazilian surfer Rodrigo Koxa rides a 24.38-meter (80-foot) wave at Nazaré, breaking McNamara's 2011 record. The wave is certified by Guinness in 2018. The record standard becomes: all modern big-wave records are set at Nazaré.

timeline.title: Sebastian Steudtner sets the current world record
timeline.description: German surfer Sebastian Steudtner surfs a 26.21-meter (86-ft) wave at Nazaré — the current official Guinness world record for the largest wave ever ridden. Later analysis at the University of Alcalá estimated the actual height closer to 28.6 m. Steudtner is now one of the Nazaré-resident core big-wave group.

timeline.title: 100 Foot Wave (HBO) premieres
timeline.description: The documentary series 100 Foot Wave, directed by Chris Smith, premieres on HBO. Three seasons run through 2024. The show centers on McNamara and the core Nazaré group — Kai Lenny, Maya Gabeira, Pedro 'Scooby' Vianna, Justine Dupont — and makes Nazaré's story available to a non-surf general audience for the first time. It wins four Primetime Emmy Awards.

timeline.title: WSL Nazaré Tow Challenge
timeline.description: The World Surf League runs its first official Tow Surfing Challenge at Nazaré — competitive big-wave surfing with jet-ski-assisted entry. Top male and female surfers from across the big-wave circuit. Becomes an annual event during the October-to-February swell window.

timeline.title: Nazaré named a UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage candidate
timeline.description: The traditional Nazaré fisherwomen's dress (sete saias) and the estendal sardine-drying practice are formally submitted to UNESCO for consideration as protected Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity. The Portuguese Ministry of Culture leads the file.

zones.name: Praia do Norte
zones.character: The big-wave beach. 1.5 km of sand directly below the Sítio headland. October–February the waves here routinely reach 8–15 m; on peak swells, 20+ m. Outside swell season (April–September) it can be a sleepy empty beach with 3-meter shore break — still not great for casual swimming. Parking lot at the northern end gives the best view of the break.
zones.best_for: Watching the big waves from the cliff (the Forte viewpoint is directly above); surfing only if you are at the top of the sport; photography
zones.notes: Do NOT swim at Praia do Norte. The rip currents are lethal even in summer.

zones.name: Praia da Nazaré (village beach)
zones.character: The working fishing beach in front of the old town. Protected by the Sítio headland to the north. Calm water, gentle slope, the wooden drying racks (estendal) for sardines are the iconic feature. The beach is used by both tourists and fishermen; there are dedicated areas for fishing-boat pulls.
zones.best_for: Family swimming, photographing the sardine-drying racks, lunch at the kiosks, fish market right behind
zones.notes: The estendal — the wooden racks with fish drying in the sun — are a working practice, not a display. Ask before photographing the women working them.

zones.name: Sítio (upper town)
zones.character: The clifftop neighborhood — 120 m above the lower village. Reached by the Funicular, the 175-step Capuchinhos stair, or winding road. Home to the Santuário da Nossa Senhora, the Forte de São Miguel Arcanjo lighthouse, the Surf Museum, and the café-filled main square.
zones.best_for: The best viewpoint in town. The 4 p.m. cliff promenade. Sunday mass at the sanctuary.
zones.notes: In winter during big-wave sessions, the cliff at the Forte fills with hundreds of spectators. Arrive by 11 a.m.

landmarks.name: Forte de São Miguel Arcanjo (the lighthouse)
landmarks.description: 16th-century fortress at the tip of the Sítio headland, converted to a lighthouse in 1903 and surf museum in 2017. The white-and-red structure visible in every famous Nazaré big-wave photograph. Admission €2; open year-round.

landmarks.name: Santuário de Nossa Senhora da Nazaré
landmarks.description: 14th-century Marian sanctuary on the Sítio clifftop. Houses the miracle-story 4th-century Byzantine statue of Our Lady of Nazareth. Annual pilgrimage September 8. The church is free; the museum attached is €2.

landmarks.name: Funicular da Nazaré
landmarks.description: Victorian-era inclined funicular connecting the lower village to Sítio. Originally water-counterweighted, now electric. 318 meters long, 120-meter vertical rise. Runs every 10 min; €2.20 round trip.

landmarks.name: Ermida da Memória
landmarks.description: The tiny Memory Chapel at the exact spot on the Sítio cliff where, according to the Lenda, Dom Fuas Roupinho's horse stopped. Built immediately after the miracle; rebuilt in 1377. Contains a relief carving of the legend.

landmarks.name: Estendal (sardine-drying racks)
landmarks.description: The wooden racks where fisherwomen sun-dry sardines in open air along the Praia da Nazaré. A continuously-practiced traditional fishery technique, now submitted to UNESCO as Intangible Cultural Heritage.

landmarks.name: Praia do Norte viewpoint
landmarks.description: The cliff edge at the back of the Forte parking lot — the spot from which every iconic big-wave photograph of Nazaré is taken. On swell days there is no railing between you and a 50-meter drop; go carefully.

cultural_refs.title: 100 Foot Wave
cultural_refs.creator: Chris Smith (HBO)
cultural_refs.description: Three-season HBO documentary series following Garrett McNamara and the Nazaré big-wave community. Four Primetime Emmy Awards. The show that brought Nazaré's story to a general international audience. Seasons air in summer of each year.

cultural_refs.title: The Big Wave (Nazaré, Portugal)
cultural_refs.creator: Various documentaries
cultural_refs.description: Multiple short documentary films and National Geographic segments document the McNamara era. The 2013 IMAX film 'Storm Surfers 3D' includes major Nazaré sequences.

cultural_refs.title: Rise of the Giants
cultural_refs.creator: Dana Brown
cultural_refs.description: A 2024 full-length documentary on Nazaré's current generation of women big-wave surfers — Maya Gabeira, Justine Dupont, Michelle des Bouillons. Premieres at Tribeca.

cultural_refs.title: Hound of the Sea
cultural_refs.creator: Garrett McNamara with Karen Karbo
cultural_refs.description: McNamara's memoir. Chapter 14 is about the first Nazaré email from Dino Casimiro and the 2011 ride. One of the defining primary-source documents of the modern big-wave era.

cultural_refs.title: Mar Alto
cultural_refs.creator: Camané
cultural_refs.description: Portuguese fado singer Camané's album of fado do mar (sea fado) — the sub-genre of Portuguese fado that deals with fishing-village life. 'Quando as mulheres viúvas do Nazaré' ('When the widows of Nazaré') is the canonical Nazaré fado.

cultural_refs.title: Portuguese Irregular Verbs & the Nazaré chapter
cultural_refs.creator: Alexander McCall Smith
cultural_refs.description: The minor Nazaré chapter in McCall Smith's Portuguese-set comic novel is the most widely-read English-language literary treatment of the village before the 2011 McNamara ride — a baseline for how globally-obscure Nazaré was, in Anglophone readers' minds, until a decade ago.

cultural_refs.title: The Lenda da Nazaré
cultural_refs.creator: Medieval Portuguese oral tradition
cultural_refs.description: The founding story of the town — Dom Fuas Roupinho, his horse stopping at the cliff edge, the Marian intervention. Inscribed in the Ermida da Memória chapel. Every Portuguese schoolchild learns this.

cultural_refs.title: 'Go to Portugal' tourism campaign
cultural_refs.creator: Turismo de Portugal
cultural_refs.description: Portugal's national tourism agency has used the Nazaré wave image as the centerpiece of its international advertising since about 2015. The visual — wave plus lighthouse — is now Portugal's most-used tourism photograph.

cultural_refs.title: WSL Big Wave Awards
cultural_refs.creator: World Surf League
cultural_refs.description: Since 2022, the WSL's Big Wave Tow-In Challenge has been held annually at Nazaré during the October–February swell season. The competition is now the primary global big-wave event.

cultural_refs.title: Rafael Bordalo Pinheiro's Nazarené ceramics
cultural_refs.creator: Rafael Bordalo Pinheiro
cultural_refs.description: The iconic ceramic figurines of Nazarené fisherwomen in seven skirts, still made at the Fábrica de Faianças das Caldas da Rainha. These are the small painted figures you see in every Portuguese souvenir shop. They come from here.

cultural_refs.title: Fado do mar
cultural_refs.creator: Various Portuguese fadistas
cultural_refs.description: A sub-genre of fado — Portugal's national music — devoted to the emotional weight of fishing-village life. Canonical Nazaré fados include 'O Barco Negro' (Amália Rodrigues, 1954) and 'A Casa da Mariquinhas' (Alfredo Marceneiro). The genre's central theme — *saudade* for men who went to sea and did not return — is Nazaré history in song.

cultural_refs.title: Livro do Desassossego (Book of Disquiet)
cultural_refs.creator: Fernando Pessoa
cultural_refs.description: Pessoa — Portugal's greatest modern writer — visited Nazaré as a young man and referenced its coast in his ocean-inflected writing. His descriptions of the Atlantic light, the fishermen's wives, and the sanctuary bells shape the Portuguese literary image of the village for the 20th century.

cultural_refs.title: Nazaré (1952 film)
cultural_refs.creator: Manuel Guimarães
cultural_refs.description: Early Portuguese sound film shot entirely in Nazaré — a neorealist portrait of the fishing community and the seven-skirt women. A significant record of the pre-tourism village. Preserved at Cinemateca Portuguesa.

cultural_refs.title: Nossa Senhora da Nazaré pilgrimage
cultural_refs.creator: Catholic tradition since 1182
cultural_refs.description: The Marian pilgrimage centered on the cliff-top sanctuary. On September 8 the Nossa Senhora da Nazaré statue is processed through the village streets. ~100,000 attendees each year — comparable to the annual surf crowd but twelve months older. One of the oldest continuously-celebrated Marian pilgrimages in Iberia.

cultural_refs.title: Portuguese salt-cod trade (bacalhau)
cultural_refs.creator: Portuguese Atlantic fishery, 15th c. onward
cultural_refs.description: Nazaré's economic historical relationship with *bacalhau* — salt cod — is 500 years old. Portuguese fishing fleets reached Newfoundland in the 1490s; Nazaré was one of the ports that processed and dried the catch. The town's bacalhau dishes (bacalhau à Nazaré, bacalhau assado) are part of the 365-recipes-per-year Portuguese cod tradition.

recurring_events.name: Big Wave Season
recurring_events.description: The window during which the Atlantic winter swells line up with the Nazaré Canyon and the biggest rides of the year happen. Not a scheduled event — an entire season. Daily forecasts are published at magicseaweed.com and surfline.com. On peak days hundreds of spectators line the Forte cliff.

recurring_events.name: WSL Nazaré Tow Challenge
recurring_events.when_text: One-day event scheduled during Oct–Feb swell window
recurring_events.description: The World Surf League's competitive big-wave event. Invited surfers + partner water-safety team per rider. Broadcast live worldwide. Exact date announced 72 hours in advance depending on the forecast.

recurring_events.name: Festas da Senhora da Nazaré
recurring_events.description: The major annual Marian pilgrimage — attendance approaches 100,000 across the week. Processions from the Ermida da Memória to the Santuário, folkloric performances, fireworks over the Atlantic. The biggest event in Nazaré's domestic-tourism calendar.

recurring_events.name: Carnaval da Nazaré
recurring_events.description: Portugal's Atlantic-coast carnival — local krewes (ranchos) parade in the Sítio and the lower village. Much smaller than Rio or Venice; culturally specific; foreign tourists minimal.

recurring_events.name: Feira do Livro
recurring_events.description: Summer book fair on the Avenida Marginal. Portuguese-language books, local authors. Part of Nazaré's self-positioning as a cultured destination outside its big-wave months.

businesses.name: Museu do Surf de Nazaré (Forte de São Miguel)
businesses.description: Surf museum inside the 16th-century fortress/lighthouse. Exhibits on the canyon, the big-wave era, McNamara and Gabeira's equipment. €2. Free guided tours at 11 a.m. and 3 p.m. in summer.
businesses.address: Forte de São Miguel Arcanjo, Sítio, Nazaré

businesses.name: Taberna d'Adélia
businesses.description: Family-run seafood tavern in the Praia (lower village). Caldeirada de peixe, sardinhas assadas, the Nazaré bacalhau. No reservations; expect a wait in summer. €20–35 per person.

businesses.name: Rosa dos Ventos
businesses.description: Seafood on Avenida Marginal with an Atlantic view. Whole grilled fish (sargo, robalo, dourada), good wine list. €30–45 pp.

businesses.name: Kiosk & pastelaria Miradouro
businesses.description: Cliff-top café at the Sítio belvedere. Local pastries (pastéis de nata + the Nazaré-specific nicknack nazaré), coffee with a view. €4–8. The right café for an afternoon between big-wave sets.

businesses.name: Santuário de Nossa Senhora da Nazaré
businesses.description: The 14th-century Marian sanctuary, with attached small museum displaying ex-voto offerings from nine centuries of pilgrims. Free to enter the church; museum €2.

businesses.name: Funicular da Nazaré
businesses.description: The 1889 inclined funicular. €2.20 round trip. Primary way to get between Praia and Sítio without walking the 175-step Capuchinhos stairway.

businesses.name: Estendal de Peixe (the drying racks)
businesses.description: The wooden sardine-drying racks on the Praia da Nazaré. Not a business in the conventional sense — a working practice with dozens of fisherwomen. Sardines €6–10 per plate.
